This page is the contract for the Caracal documentation system. It defines the route layout, page schema, AI route schema, and the writing instructions every page must follow. Anyone authoring or reviewing a page must apply these rules.

Learn how to deploy, operate, integrate, and verify Caracal through runtime guides, SDK references, security material, and machine-readable docs. Open quickstartRead overview

AGENT DOCS // MACHINE MODE This route is optimized for agents, retrieval systems, and tooling. It is not a prose version of the human docs. Pages are structured as deterministic contracts with stable headings and compact operational fields.

Field Meaning canonical_human Human source page for this machine-readable page. last_verified Date the AI contract was checked against the source page. source_files Repository files used as evidence for the contract. Inputs Required and conditional values an agent must supply. Outputs Values the operation or concept returns. Constraints Non-negotiable invariants and failure conditions. Steps Deterministic execution or evaluation order. Errors Machine-usable failure classes and remediation.

/ai/open-source/end-users/ — operator contracts. /ai/open-source/sdk/ — integration contracts. /ai/open-source/developers/ — implementation contracts. /ai/enterprise/ — enterprise contracts. /ai/resources/ — shared vocabulary contracts.

Prefer AI pages for parsing, extraction, indexing, and agent planning. Prefer human pages for narrative explanation and learning flow. Treat canonical_human as the source route when a conflict is detected. Treat missing required inputs as a terminal planning error.

Every AI page should use this ordered section model: Definition Inputs Outputs Constraints Steps Usage rules Errors Examples See also See /resources/documentation-system/structure for the documentation system contract.

Caracal Enterprise keeps OSS broker execution separate from CCLE gateway and vault execution. The OSS runtime must not import Enterprise modules, read CCLE_* settings, own gateway provider records, or reach Enterprise vault credentials directly.

OSS owns local broker runtime, OSS vault adapters, policy evaluation contracts, and SDK-facing CCL behavior. Enterprise owns CCLE API routes, gateway provider registry records, gateway_vault provider credentials, metering ingestion, denial compliance events, and dashboard operations. Shared behavior must cross through audited bridge code or stable authority contracts, not direct runtime imports. Provider credentials are stored and resolved by Enterprise gateway/vault paths. Authenticated providers use gateway_vault; unauthenticated providers use none.

In production, the Enterprise gateway fails closed unless provider registry enforcement, revocation sync, quota enforcement, secret binding, and their runtime dependencies are available. A production process with those features disabled or missing dependencies must not start. Operational checks: Confirm gateway health before routing traffic. Verify provider registry cache invalidation after provider URL, status, or credential changes. Treat missing workspace or organization context as a gateway denial. Review gateway denial events in compliance reports when investigating rejected traffic. Do not route Enterprise gateway requests with X-Caracal-Target-URL; use X-Caracal-Provider-ID so the registry builds the upstream URL.

Provider base URLs must be HTTPS public FQDNs. The registry rejects userinfo, IP literals, loopback/private-looking hosts, query strings, and fragments. Allowed paths are segment bounded: /v1 permits /v1/models, but not /v10/models. Encoded traversal segments such as %2e%2e are rejected.

Secrets and CCLE keys are explicit reveal flows, not ambient page data: Provider secrets are revealed through the Enterprise API reveal endpoint and clear from the UI on hide or timeout. CCLE plaintext API keys are returned only to admin users; non-admin users receive key status and masked metadata. Rotate and revoke actions require admin authority. Logs, compliance metadata, and denial events must not include plaintext secret values.

Dashboard sections are route-backed so operators can deep-link directly to the working view they need: /dashboard/gateway overview /dashboard/gateway/clusters /dashboard/gateway/routing /dashboard/gateway/logs /dashboard/gateway/quota /dashboard/gateway/revocation /dashboard/settings/general /dashboard/settings/security /dashboard/settings/api /dashboard/settings/notifications /dashboard/settings/billing /dashboard/settings/danger Removed or hidden controls must not imply unavailable behavior. If a workflow is not API-backed and usable, either wire it to a real endpoint or remove it from the UI.

Before marking this phase complete: Run the OSS/Enterprise separation scan or equivalent import tests. Run provider registry, gateway fail-closed, secret storage, CCLE credential disclosure, and compliance aggregation tests. Run dashboard component tests for gateway, analytics, setup, secrets, providers, compliance, ledger, principals, policies, delegation, and mandates. Run the Enterprise frontend production build. Review logs and compliance output for plaintext secret values.

Split OSS broker and Enterprise gateway/vault execution paths so OSS runtime code no longer owns CCLE settings, gateway providers, or Enterprise credential lookup. Added Enterprise gateway fail-closed startup validation, provider registry URL hardening, segment-bounded provider path allowlists, and gateway denial compliance events. Limited CCLE plaintext API key disclosure to admin users while preserving masked key status for non-admin users. Reworked Enterprise dashboard gateway and settings into physical subroutes, removed placeholder-only UI controls, and added an analytics offline state for unreachable Enterprise API responses. Added regression coverage for credential storage modes, secret reveal behavior, gateway production startup, compliance aggregation, CCLE credential disclosure, provider SSRF controls, dashboard routes, and production frontend builds. Authority enforcement in Caracal is pre-execution decision gating: actions are evaluated against explicit authority context before side effects run.

Caracal treats delegated action execution as a security boundary. The model is built so an operation is not considered valid just because it is syntactically correct or technically reachable. It must also be authorized in the right scope, with the right identity context, and with controls that fail closed when required context is missing. In practical terms, the model connects these elements: identity and principal context policy constraints mandate/delegation scope runtime verification checks audit reconstruction capability

Host entrypoint orchestration and runtime command boundaries (caracal host mode vs in-container mode). Runtime preflight and hard-cut checks that reject unsafe configuration in protected paths. CLI and SDK operations where authority-relevant identifiers are created, mutated, and validated. Threat analysis and incident triage where decisions must be replayable from logs and state.
documents.s: Where this appears in the system

documents.t: Pre-execution enforcement A request path should reject invalid authority context before provider-side action execution. Fail-closed behavior Missing critical security context does not degrade to permissive execution in protected runtime paths. Canonical scope handling Workspace and identity scope must remain explicit through command/API boundaries. Auditability Decisions and transitions should be reconstructable for incident review.

Scope confusion is a primary failure mode: if principal/workspace/policy context is ambiguous, deny and require explicit context. Configuration drift can invalidate security posture: runtime checks should reject prohibited modes rather than warn. Cross-workspace assumptions are invalid unless explicitly modeled by authority primitives. Provider availability is not equivalent to authorization success; connectivity checks do not replace policy enforcement.

Keep security-sensitive values explicit and environment-scoped. Fail fast on invalid configuration that changes security posture. Validate configuration changes through runtime health checks and read paths.

Allowlist configuration. Resource access pattern rules and defaults. Allowlists are deny-by-default. When allowlist enforcement is enabled, a principal without an active matching allowlist entry is denied at runtime.

Install the host caracal command, then let it run the runtime stack for you. Audience: User Context: This page covers the open-source install path needed to run the containerized runtime from a local machine.

Caracal is container-first. The local installation gives you a host command that orchestrates Docker Compose. PostgreSQL, Redis, the mcp service, the CLI session, and Flow all run in containers. That means installation has two parts: install caracal-core, or use a source checkout, to get the host caracal command make sure Docker with Compose support is available on the host Package names are intentionally split by role: Package Use it for caracal-core Host-side Python runtime orchestrator. This is the package that provides the caracal command. caracal-server Python runtime server modules installed inside the runtime container image. End users normally do not install this directly on the host. caracal-sdk Standalone Python SDK for applications that call a running Caracal runtime. It does not provide the host caracal command. @caracal/core Node.js / TypeScript SDK for applications that call a running Caracal runtime. It is unrelated to the Python caracal-core package despite the similar name.
Python 3.10 or newer Docker with docker compose enough local permissions to start containers and create local state directories

The repository-supported install path for local use is a source checkout with uv: git clone https://github.com/Garudex-Labs/Caracal.git cd Caracal uv venv source .venv/bin/activate uv sync --locked caracal --help If you are contributing, the repository also documents make setup-dev, but the steps above are the direct underlying workflow. After installation, verify the host command can resolve Docker Compose: docker compose version caracal --help Before starting the runtime, create $CCL_HOME/runtime/.env with owner-only permissions and the required secure defaults: export CCL_HOME="${CCL_HOME:-$HOME/.caracal}" mkdir -p "$CCL_HOME/runtime" umask 077 cat > "$CCL_HOME/runtime/.env" <<EOF CCL_DB_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -hex 24) CCL_REDIS_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -hex 24) CCL_VAULT_TOKEN=dev-local-token VAULT_AUTH_SECRET=$(openssl rand -hex 32) VAULT_ENC_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 16) CCL_ENV_MODE=dev CCL_ALLOW_INTERNAL_PROVIDER_URLS=true EOF chmod 600 "$CCL_HOME/runtime/.env" Then run caracal bootstrap, caracal up, and eval "$(caracal auth token --format env)".

The runtime resolves state under CCL_HOME. If CCL_HOME is unset, Caracal falls back to ~/.caracal. Important paths: ~/.caracal/workspaces/ for workspace directories ~/.caracal/keystore/ for local encryption and signing material ~/.caracal/system/history/ for CLI history and system metadata When the runtime starts from the packaged image compose file, the container uses /home/caracal/.caracal internally and mounts host-shared I/O under /caracal-host-io.

At startup, the host command in caracal/runtime/entrypoints.py tries to locate a compose file in the repository or current directory. If it cannot find one, it writes and uses an embedded compose file that defines: postgres redis mcp cli flow This is why a local install does not require you to provision PostgreSQL or Redis separately for the standard open-source path.

caracal on the host will fail early if docker compose version is not available. The host command can use a custom compose file through --compose-file or CCL_COMPOSE_FILE, but it must contain the expected runtime services. If you install from source, local development compose files may use local image builds; packaged installs may use image-based compose instead.
Bring up a local Caracal runtime, open Flow, verify health through host commands, and execute one SDK connectivity check in under ten minutes.
Python 3.10+ with caracal-core available in the active environment. Docker with Compose support (docker compose). Network access to pull runtime images when running caracal up without --no-pull.

Run: caracal --help docker compose version Verification criteria: caracal --help prints subcommands. docker compose version returns successfully. If either command fails, fix host environment setup before proceeding.
documents.s: Step 1: Verify host command and Docker Compose

documents.t: Run: export CCL_HOME="${CCL_HOME:-$HOME/.caracal}" mkdir -p "$CCL_HOME/runtime" umask 077 cat > "$CCL_HOME/runtime/.env" <<EOF CCL_DB_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -hex 24) CCL_REDIS_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -hex 24) CCL_VAULT_TOKEN=dev-local-token VAULT_AUTH_SECRET=$(openssl rand -hex 32) VAULT_ENC_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 16) CCL_ENV_MODE=dev CCL_ALLOW_INTERNAL_PROVIDER_URLS=true EOF chmod 600 "$CCL_HOME/runtime/.env" Expected behavior: $CCL_HOME/runtime/.env exists with owner-only permissions. Redis, database, local vault bootstrap secrets, and explicit dev relaxations are set before Compose starts.
documents.s: Step 2: Create the secure local runtime env

documents.t: caracal bootstrap caracal up Expected behavior: Bootstrap creates the system principal, grants admin/dev setup capabilities, and issues the first AIS nonce. Runtime stack starts with postgres, redis, vault, and mcp services. Compose startup completes without non-zero exit codes. Optional variant: caracal up --no-pull Use --no-pull only when required images are already present locally.
documents.s: Step 3: Bootstrap and start runtime services

documents.t: Run: eval "$(caracal auth token --format env)" Verification criteria: CCL_SESS_TOKEN is set in the current shell. The token was minted through the local AIS Unix socket.

Run: caracal flow Expected behavior: Flow launches inside the runtime container. You can navigate screens without immediate runtime connection errors. caracal with no args also routes to Flow by default on host entrypoints.

Run: caracal logs -f Optional service filtering: caracal logs postgres redis mcp Verification criteria: Log stream is active. No repeated fatal startup failures. Then open restricted container CLI: caracal cli You are now in the in-container command boundary for authority operations.
documents.s: Step 6: Validate runtime logs and command boundary

documents.t: Minimal SDK check: import os from caracal_sdk.client import CaracalClient client = CaracalClient(api_key=os.environ["CCL_SESS_TOKEN"]) print("client-initialized") client.close() Verification criteria: Client initialization succeeds when the AIS-issued session token and runtime URL are valid. No SDKConfigurationError is raised. The SDK resolves base URL from CCL_API_URL or defaults to http://localhost:${CCL_API_PORT:-8080}.

Caracal Open Source is the self-managed Caracal runtime and host orchestrator for pre-execution authority enforcement.

The Open Source edition provides the host command entrypoint (caracal) and the runtime modules that execute authority checks before actions run. In this repository, that split is visible as: caracal-core (packages/caracal) for host orchestration commands. caracal-server (packages/caracal-server) for runtime server modules installed in the runtime container image. The runtime host command set includes up, down, reset, purge, logs, cli, flow, and migrate. The up path starts the local stack services (postgres, redis, mcp). Open Source and Enterprise use different environment-variable namespaces: CCL_ for Open Source core configuration. CCLE_ for Enterprise-specific configuration.

Package boundary: packages/caracal/pyproject.toml defines caracal-core and maps the caracal script to caracal.runtime.entrypoints:caracal_entrypoint. Runtime boundary: packages/caracal-server/pyproject.toml defines caracal-server as runtime server modules installed only in the runtime container image. Runtime implementation: packages/caracal/caracal/runtime/ contains host entrypoints, runtime data, and hard-cut preflight checks. Server implementation: packages/caracal-server/caracal/ contains CLI, core authority logic, identity, storage, flow, monitoring, provider, and enterprise modules.
documents.s: Where this appears in the system

documents.t: Hard-cut is an operating requirement in runtime enforcement paths, not a user-facing mode switch in docs. In hard-cut runtime paths, SQLite is rejected and PostgreSQL is required. In hard-cut runtime paths, the secret backend must be explicitly managed (vault) and local/file-backed secret backends are rejected. In hard-cut runtime paths, local vault mode values (local, dev, development) are rejected. This documentation set describes self-managed operation from this repository and runtime stack commands. It does not define a hosted Caracal service.

This rulebook defines how Caracal documentation is written, split, linked, and maintained. Audience: Documentation maintainers, contributors, and reviewers. Context: Apply these rules before writing or editing any page in the Caracal docs system.

Write from the codebase, not from assumptions. Explain what exists, why it exists, how it works, and when to use it. Keep one canonical explanation for each concept. Prefer exact behavior over conceptual hand-waving. Use repository terminology exactly as the code and UI use it. Separate operator guidance from contributor guidance. Document system boundaries, not just happy-path tasks. Treat missing detail as a documentation defect.

Every content page must follow this order unless a section is genuinely not applicable: Title One-line summary Audience Context Core explanation Internal behavior Practical usage Edge cases and constraints Related concepts Use short introductory metadata lines near the top of the page: Audience: user | developer | both Context: where this page fits in the system

Use host command for the outer caracal command that orchestrates Docker Compose. Use in-container CLI for the interactive Click CLI that runs inside the runtime container. Use Flow for the terminal UI implemented under caracal/flow. Use workspace for the directory rooted under CCL_HOME/workspaces/<name>. Use principal for any identity that can hold authority. Use authority policy for issuance constraints. Use execution mandate or mandate for signed, time-bound authorization. Use delegation edge for graph relationships between mandates. Use authority ledger for mandate issuance, validation, and revocation events. Use ledger for metering and resource-usage events. Use provider-scoped scope for provider:<provider>:resource:<id> and provider:<provider>:action:<id> values. Do not alternate between synonyms such as agent identity and principal, or token and mandate, unless the code defines them as different objects.

Define a concept once on its canonical page and link back to it elsewhere. Link to the narrowest page that fully answers the next question. Use internal links heavily when a page depends on another concept. Do not duplicate definitions just to avoid linking. Use related-links sections to connect adjacent concepts, not to compensate for weak structure.

Use diagrams only when a flow is easier to understand visually than textually. Keep diagrams small and system-level. Prefer text diagrams or simple mermaid diagrams. Each diagram must explain an actual code path or user flow. Do not add decorative diagrams. Valid example: Host caracal command -> Docker Compose runtime -> mcp service / cli session / flow session -> PostgreSQL + Redis + workspace state

Avoid code snippets by default. Include code only when a reader must copy a command, config fragment, or exact invocation. Every example must match the current codebase and command surface. Prefer the smallest successful example first. Put extended or advanced examples in collapsible sections when they would break the main flow. Never add illustrative code that is not needed to operate or understand the system.

Use short paragraphs and descriptive headings. Use tables when comparing commands, modules, or behaviors. Use callouts only when they clarify risk, constraints, or failure modes. Use monospace for commands, file paths, environment variables, identifiers, and config keys. Keep lists flat unless nesting is essential to correctness. Keep prose technical, precise, and direct.

Open-source docs must stay split between End Users and Developers. End-user pages explain installation, operation, workflows, configuration, troubleshooting, and public security behavior. Developer pages explain architecture, module boundaries, internal flows, test strategy, release surfaces, and contributor workflow. Enterprise pages remain user-facing placeholders only in the public docs. SDK pages may define structure now, but SDK content stays deferred until the SDK content phase. The only enterprise-adjacent public developer page is Enterprise Connector, and it must describe public connector boundaries only.

Do not do any of the following: Do not use emojis anywhere. Do not use vague explanations. Do not repeat explanations across pages. Do not write shallow summaries that omit system behavior. Do not add unnecessary code snippets. Do not use decorative writing. Do not mix user guidance and contributor internals on the same page. Do not describe enterprise internals in public documentation. Do not hide critical constraints inside long paragraphs. Do not write around missing understanding of the code.
No emojis anywhere. No vague explanations. No repeated explanations across pages. No shallow summaries. No unnecessary code snippets. No decorative writing. No marketing tone. No filler text.

Before a page is complete, confirm all of the following: The intended audience is explicit. The page has one clear purpose. The canonical definition lives on exactly one page. Commands and config examples match the current implementation. Internal behavior is explained where it matters. Constraints and failure modes are explicit. Related pages point to the canonical concept source. The page does not depend on undocumented prerequisite behavior.

This page is the contract for the Caracal documentation system. It defines the route layout, page schema, AI route schema, and the writing instructions every page must follow. Anyone authoring or reviewing a page must apply these rules.

The site has two parallel routes that share the same source of truth: Human route: /<section>/<page> — readable, learning-oriented. AI route: /ai/<section>/<page> — instruction-first, retrieval-optimized. Every human page that has a meaningful machine version declares ai_counterpart in its front matter. Every AI page declares canonical_human in its front matter. AI pages are not indexed in human search and do not appear in human sidebars.

Path Audience Edition / All readers Both /open-source/ Integrators, operators, contributors OSS /enterprise/ Integrators, reviewers Enterprise /resources/ All readers Both /build, /manage, /reference, /resources Hub orientation pages Both /ai/ Machine readers Both

Every page is exactly one of: concept — explains a term or model. task — accomplishes a single user goal. reference — exhaustive, stable lookup material. hub — short orientation page with a child index. tutorial — multi-step learning path. Used only for the quickstart.

--- title: <Title> sidebar_label: <Short label> slug: <stable URL slug> page_type: concept | task | reference | hub | tutorial audience: integrator | reviewer | contributor | operator | all edition: oss | enterprise | both version: 1.0 status: stub | authored ai_counterpart: /ai/... # required when an AI mirror exists ---

--- id: ai-<stable-id> title: <Title> slug: /ai/... canonical_human: /... applies_to: [oss] | [enterprise] | [oss, enterprise] edition: oss | enterprise | both audience: ai page_type: concept | task | reference version: 1.0 status: stub | authored last_verified: YYYY-MM-DD source_files: - path/to/file.py ---

Pick the block that matches page_type. Omit any section that is genuinely empty. concept One-line definition (verbatim glossary entry where applicable). Plain-language explanation. Where this concept appears in the system. Constraints and edge cases. Related concepts. task Goal. Prerequisites. Ordered steps. Verification. Related pages. reference Scope. Reference table or list. Notes on stability and versioning. tutorial Outcome. Prerequisites. Linear walkthrough with verification at each step. Where to go next. hub One-paragraph orientation. Bulleted index of child pages with one-line summaries.

Use these section headers in order. Omit empty ones. ## Definition — one sentence. ## Inputs — table: name, type, required, source, notes. ## Outputs — table: name, type, notes. ## Constraints — bullet list of invariants and limits. ## Steps — numbered, deterministic procedure. ## Usage rules — do / do not bullets. ## Errors — table: code, meaning, remediation. ## Examples — minimal runnable snippets. ## See also — related AI pages first, human pages second.

Reflect current product behavior only. No marketing language. No emojis, no em-dashes, no filler. Cite only the files listed in front matter. Keep pages short. Split when content exceeds one screen. One canonical topic per page. Cross-link instead of restating. Hard-cut is the system's operating principle. Do not present it as a configurable mode.

Expand internal terms on first use, for example "AI Service runtime isolation (AIS)". The glossary at /resources/glossary is the single canonical definition source. Every concept page's first paragraph must equal its glossary entry verbatim.

Edit the human page. Update the AI counterpart in the same change. Bump last_verified on the AI page. Update source_files if code references changed. CI fails when a human page commit is newer than its AI counterpart's last_verified.

Choose the canonical path under the correct section. Create <path>.mdx (or <path>/index.mdx for hubs). Decide whether an AI counterpart is needed. Concepts, tasks, and references typically need one. Tutorials and marketing-style overviews do not. If an AI counterpart is needed, create /ai/<path>.md using the AI schema. Add the page to the relevant sidebar in src/theme/DocSidebar/shared.ts.
Update every cross-link. Remove the AI counterpart in the same change. Add a redirect when the slug must change.

Pages are filled in this order so the most read content is correct first. Landing. OSS Overview. Quickstart and Installation. Concepts hub and the four most-referenced concepts: principal, policy, mandate, workspace. SDK Overview, Python SDK Usage, Python API Surface, Tool ID Grammar. CLI hub and Host Commands reference. Configuration overview and Environment Variables reference. Security overview and Threat Model digest. Remaining concepts: delegation, caveat, ledger, intent, AIS, MEK, broker vs gateway, authority enforcement model. Remaining CLI command pages, configuration block references, workflows. Node SDK pages (mirror of Python). Troubleshooting. Developers track. Enterprise track. AI mirrors are filled in lockstep with each human page.
